title: Help & Glossary
subtitle: Reference for Richman decision cards, analysis dimensions, and push rules.

blocks.text: Every decision card shows a change badge in the top-right corner. The backend computes the badge type by diffing the current analysis against the previous one, so a glance at the color tells you what kind of change happened. When multiple conditions match, the highest-priority badge wins.

rows: At least one data source is degraded; analysis marked degraded = true
This holding has no previous analysis to compare with
Action aggressiveness level went up (e.g. hold → add)
Action aggressiveness level went down (e.g. add → hold)
At least one dimension flipped direction but the action did not change
Action and dimensions unchanged, but execution steps or target position changed

blocks.text: Priority order (highest wins): Data degraded → First analysis → Action upgrade / downgrade → Signal flip → Plan adjust → Confidence shift.
Action aggressiveness levels used for upgrade / downgrade detection:

items: 2 Aggressive add
1 Small add
0 Hold and wait
-1 Gradual reduce
-2 Control position

blocks.text: Upgrade = new level > old level. Downgrade = new level < old level.

Richman splits each holding's analysis into three independent dimensions. Each dimension produces its own direction (bullish / bearish / neutral) and a short summary of quantitative signals. A synthesis step then weights the three into a final recommendation.

rows: Moving averages, RSI, MACD, momentum score
PE percentile, PB percentile, northbound flow
Events that may move the price soon
Polymarket events, keyword matching, LLM search summary

blocks.text: On a decision card each dimension renders as a colored chip: green for bullish, red for bearish, gray for neutral. When a dimension flips direction versus the previous analysis, the chip adds an arrow and strikes through the old value so you can immediately see which dimension drove the change.
The detail page's Three Dimensions section expands each dimension into its full signal list, weight adjustment trace (X% → Y%), and a short written conclusion.
Every decision card produces one recommended action out of five possible levels. The numeric aggressiveness level is the same value used by the badge engine to detect upgrades and downgrades.

rows: Very clear signal; raise the position substantially
Positive bias; raise the position modestly
Neutral or insufficient signal; keep current position
Signal weakening; lower the position in steps
Risk is significant; cut the position to a low level

blocks.text: Actions pair with execution plans: add / reduce actions usually ship with a multi-step staged plan, while hold-and-wait goes with monitor mode (stop-loss and take-profit lines only).

An execution plan breaks the recommended action into concrete steps. Each step has a trigger condition and a rationale. The card only shows the first 3 steps; if there are more, a 'view details' link expands the full list on the detail page.

rows: Adding or reducing position in multiple batches
Ordered steps, each with its own trigger and delta
Only one step; used for small position tweaks
No steps; a stop-loss and a take-profit line are given instead

blocks.text: There are three kinds of triggers:

items: Price: fires when the price matches a condition (<= or >= a level)
Time: fires after a specific moment or window
Event: fires after an external event (Fed meeting, earnings, Polymarket settlement, etc.)

blocks.text: Every plan has a validity window (valid_days). The plan is automatically re-analyzed when the window expires, or immediately if a stop-loss or take-profit condition triggers within the window.

Confidence is an integer from 0 to 100 expressing how trustworthy the current conclusion is. It is derived from signal agreement across the three dimensions, data completeness, and the weight distribution.

items: When all three dimensions agree (all bullish or all bearish) confidence rises sharply
When dimensions disagree confidence drops
Any degraded data source lowers confidence
A change with |delta| >= 10 triggers a standalone Confidence shift badge

blocks.text: The PRD does not spell out the exact confidence formula; the rules above are qualitative. The 10-point threshold is documented in the #badge section.
The detail page shows confidence delta versus the previous analysis (e.g. +7 vs last) so you can see whether confidence is building or eroding.

Richman's analysis depends on several external data sources with non-overlapping responsibilities. When a source is unavailable, the affected dimensions are flagged in red in the detail page's meta column and degradation mode kicks in.

rows: Feeds Trend and Position for non A-share holdings
Global macro and policy event probabilities
Adds narrative context on top of quantitative signals

blocks.text: Exact refresh cadence depends on the backend cron schedule and is not enumerated second-by-second in the PRD. See the #push section for the three analysis windows.

When a data source the analysis depends on is unavailable, times out, or returns obviously broken data, the system drops into degradation mode instead of failing outright. A recommendation is still produced, but the card displays the Data degraded badge and the meta column explains which source degraded and why.

items: External data source timeouts or errors
Missing or clearly invalid data fields
LLM enrichment calls failing or rate-limited
Computed confidence below the safety threshold

blocks.text: Effects on the final recommendation:

items: Confidence is automatically lowered
Actions lean more conservative (closer to hold-and-wait)
Badge priority is highest so users notice immediately
The detail page meta column lists every degraded source with its reason

blocks.text: Automatic analysis runs on a backend cron across three Beijing-time windows (Asia/Shanghai), and pushes follow the same schedule. All times are Beijing time; users in other time zones should account for the offset.

rows: Overnight synthesis and the day's opening strategy
A-share close review and next-day outlook
Cross-market synthesis after the US close

blocks.text: Skip rules:

items: All windows are skipped if the user has no holdings
For a given holding, if the new analysis has no perceivable change versus the previous one (no badge), push is skipped
Channels disabled under Settings → Push Channels receive nothing
Each channel can opt in or out of individual windows

blocks.text: Risk preference is set in Settings → Account with three values: Conservative, Neutral, and Aggressive. It nudges the LLM within the weight-adjustment range; it does not change the hard rules of the analysis engine. Instead, the synthesis step biases the weights toward a more cautious or more aggressive stance.

rows: Synthesis leans on downside signals from Position and Catalyst; biased toward hold or gradual reduce
No bias; recommendations follow the raw three-dimension weights
Synthesis leans on upside signals from Trend and Catalyst; biased toward small or aggressive add

blocks.text: The exact weight-adjustment range is decided by the backend synthesis module and is not listed as a percentage in the PRD. Use the detail page's Three Dimensions section (weight trace X% → Y%) to observe the actual bias applied.
